the_trap: The source process is the legitimate signed C:\\Windows\\system32\\taskmgr.exe (EV-001: launched from explorer.exe with command line \"taskmgr.exe /4\", High integrity, corp\\Administrator), inviting under-triage as routine Task Manager activity. But the process_access requests against lsass.exe (PID 856) escalate from 0x1400 (query rights, no VM_READ; EV-002) to 0x1410 (query rights plus VM_READ 0x0010; EV-003) and then 0x1fffff (PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS; A0, whose call_trace runs through dbgcore.DLL), and the same taskmgr process (PID 4400) then writes a file named 'lsass (2).DMP' to C:\\Users\\ADMINI~1\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\2\\ (EV-004). A VM_READ-capable open of lsass followed by a lsass-named .DMP file is the signature of an LSASS memory dump. The model must not under-rate on the benign-looking signed tool; up-rank to the evidenced lsass memory dump.
rationale: taskmgr.exe (PID 4400) opens lsass.exe (PID 856) with escalating access masks — 0x1400 (query rights, no VM_READ; EV-002), 0x1410 (adds VM_READ 0x0010; EV-003), and 0x1fffff (PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS; A0) — and the same process writes 'lsass (2).DMP' into the Temp\\2 directory (EV-004, event_code 11). The A0 call_trace passing through dbgcore.DLL is consistent with a MiniDumpWriteDump-style memory dump. The escalation to VM_READ-capable access plus a same-process file_create of a lsass-named .DMP together evidence a memory dump of lsass rather than ordinary Task Manager process listing. lsass memory is where credential material resides, so this dump is consistent with credential theft. Evidenced LSASS memory dump: malicious, high; up-rank despite the signed, High-integrity tool.
